_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Altered Mental Status. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Altered Mental Status

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Altered Mental Status
points: Identify treatable causes
Perform appropriate assessment and diagnostics (e.g., oxygen saturation, glucose check, monitor)
Protect patient from complications of altered mental status (e.g., respiratory failure, shock, cardiopulmonary arrest)

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Impaired decision-making capacity
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Traumatic brain injury

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Check blood glucose immediately: treat if BGL <60 mg/dL with oral glucose (alert patient)
Airway management: head tilt-chin lift, jaw-thrust, OPA, suction
Oxygen via NRM if SpO2 <94% or altered mentation
Lateral position (recovery position) if unconscious with intact respirations
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose (premeasured) for suspected opioid overdose

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring: treat hypoglycemia as first-line
Oral glucose 25 g if BGL <60 and patient can protect airway
Naloxone 0.4-2 mg IM for suspected opioid-induced AMS
12-lead ECG acquisition to rule out cardiac cause
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)
Supraglottic airway if unable to maintain airway

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV D50W 25 g or D10W 250 mL for documented hypoglycemia
IV/IO access initiation
Naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV/IO/IN for opioid-induced AMS
IV thiamine 100 mg (before dextrose) for suspected alcohol-related or malnourished patient
Waveform capnography
IV antiemetics if vomiting risk

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: AEIOUTIPS differential: Alcohol/Acidosis, Epilepsy/Electrolytes, Insulin, Overdose/Oxygen, Uremia, Trauma/Temperature/Toxins, Psychiatric, Stroke/Shock/Seizure
12-lead ECG interpretation for cardiac cause (dysrhythmia, STEMI)
RSI and intubation for GCS ≤8 with inability to protect airway
Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV for benzodiazepine OD (caution: may precipitate withdrawal seizures)
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV for TCA-related AMS (QRS >100 ms)
Dextrose + thiamine for Wernicke encephalopathy
Stroke protocol if focal deficits, sudden onset, or FAST positive
Laboratory samples: glucose, CO oximetry, 12-lead ECG for hospital

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: With depressed mental status, initial focus is on airway protection, oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion
The violent patient may need pharmacologic and/or physical management to insure proper assessment and treatment
Hypoglycemic and hypoxic patients can be irritable and violent [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline]

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: History from bystanders and caregivers
Age of the patient
Development age and baseline functional status
Consider the following differential using the mnemonic AEIOU-TIPS:
Environment where patient found
Recent complaints (e.g., headache, chest pain, difficulty breathing, vomiting, fever)
Medical alert tags and accessory medical devices
Evaluate for reduced PO intake and/or vomiting and/or diarrhea or dehydration as a cause of AMS in the pediatric and geriatric populations

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) or AVPU description
' Baseline developmental status and change from baseline
' Temperature was taken when able
' Patient and medic safety were considered
' Pupil and neck exam were done
' Evaluation of perfusion and skin exam were performed
' IV fluids given for poor perfusion

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Hypoglycemic and hypoxic patients can be irritable and violent [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline]
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
